name: 蒸腾作用
subject: biology
curriculum_points: 植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。
阐明植物在生物圈水循环中的作用。
通过实验，探究蒸腾作用的主要器官。

excerpts.text: 植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。
阐明植物在生物圈水循环中的作用。
通过实验，探究蒸腾作用的主要器官。

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 蒸腾作用

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 生物
- **学段**: 初中
- **年级**: 七年级
- **章节**: 植物的生活
- **知识点**: 蒸腾作用
- **课标版本**: 义务教育课程标准（2022年版）

---

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
> 植物通过对水的吸收和散失参与生物圈中的水循环.

### 1.2 学业要求
> 从物质循环与能量变化的角度，阐明植物在生物圈中的作用.

### 1.3 教学提示
>

---

## 二、教学目标

### 2.1 知识与技能
- 学生能够理解蒸腾作用的基本概念和原理
- 学生能够正确进行相关计算和推理
- 学生能够识别和描述相关的概念、现象或材料
- 学生能够理解蒸腾作用的基本概念和原理

### 2.2 过程与方法
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习生物学的方法
- 学生能够运用生物学学科方法解决问题
- 学生能够在交流中表达自己的思路
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习生物学的方法

### 2.3 情感态度与价值观
- 学生感受生物学与现实生活密切相关
- 学生体验生物学探究的乐趣，树立学好生物学的信心
- 学生养成认真细致、独立思考的学习习惯
- 学生感受生物学与现实生活密切相关

---

## 三、教学重难点

### 3.1 教学重点
- 蒸腾作用的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构
- 蒸腾作用的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构

### 3.2 教学难点
- 掌握蒸腾作用的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用
- 掌握蒸腾作用的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用

## 五、教学建议（学科化补灌）

### 5.1 教学方法
- 以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「蒸腾作用」教学。
- 通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
- 围绕课标要点“植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

supplements.textbook_summary:
## 教材化讲解参考（由课标要点生成）

### 核心概念
蒸腾作用是生物学学习中的重要知识点。教学时应围绕课标要点“植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。”展开，先明确概念边界，再通过例子、图示、实验、语篇或案例帮助学生建立可迁移的理解。

### 学习路径
1. 情境导入：以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「蒸腾作用」教学。
2. 探究建构：通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
3. 应用迁移：围绕课标要点“植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

### 例题设计方向
可设计一道“识别核心概念”的基础题，一道“解释现象或文本材料”的理解题，一道“迁移到真实情境”的应用题，并要求学生说明依据。

deep_textbook_snippets.text: FIGURE 30.7 The central pith and outer cortex of the (a) flax stem are made up of parenchyma cells. Inside the cortex is a layer of sclerenchyma cells, which make up the fibers in flax rope and clothing. Humans have grown and harvested flax for thousands of years. In (b) this drawing, fourteenth-century women prepare linen. The (c) flax plant is grown and harvested for its fibers, which are used to weave linen, and for its seeds, which are the source of linseed oil. (credit a: modification of work by Emmanuel Boutet based on original work by Ryan R. MacKenzie; credit c: modification of work by Brian Dearth; scale-bar data from Matt Russell) Which layers of the stem are made of parenchyma cells? a. cortex and pith b. phloem c. sclerenchyma d. xylem Like the rest of the plant, the stem has three tissue systems: dermal, vascular, and ground tissue. Each is distinguished by characteristic cell types that perform specific tasks necessary for the plant’s growth and survival. Dermal Tissue The dermal tissue of the stem consists primarily of epidermis, a single layer of cells covering and protecting the underlying tissue. Woody plants have a tough, waterproof outer layer of cork cells commonly known as bark, which further protects the plant from damage. Epidermal cells are the most numerous and least differentiated of the cells in the epidermis. The epidermis of a leaf also contains openings known as stomata, through which the exchange of gases takes place (Figure 30.8). Two cells, known as guard cells, surround each leaf stoma, controlling its opening and closing and thus regulati

FIGURE 30.24 Visualized at 500x with a scanning electron microscope, several stomata are clearly visible on (a) the surface of this sumac (Rhus glabra) leaf. At 5,000x magnification, the guard cells of (b) a single stoma from lyre-leaved sand cress (Arabidopsis lyrata) have the appearance of lips that surround the opening. In this (c) light micrograph cross-section of an A. lyrata leaf, the guard cell pair is visible along with the large, sub-stomatal air space in the leaf. (credit: modification of work by Robert R. Wise; part c scale-bar data from Matt Russell) The epidermis is usually one cell layer thick; however, in plants that grow in very hot or very cold conditions, the epidermis may be several layers thick to protect against excessive water loss from transpiration. A waxy layer known as the cuticle covers the leaves of all plant species. The cuticle reduces the rate of water loss from the leaf surface. Other leaves may have small hairs (trichomes) on the leaf surface. Trichomes help to deter herbivory by restricting insect movements, or by storing toxic or bad-tasting compounds; they can also reduce the rate of transpiration by blocking air flow across the leaf surface (Figure 30.25). FIGURE 30.25 Trichomes give leaves a fuzzy appearance as in this (a) sundew (Drosera sp.). Leaf trichomes include (b) branched trichomes on the leaf of Arabidopsis lyrata and (c) multibranched trichomes on a mature Quercus marilandica leaf. (credit a: John Freeland; credit b, c: modification of work by Robert R. Wise; scale-bar data from Matt Russell) Below the epidermis of dicot leave

By the end of this section, you will be able to do the following: • Define water potential and explain how it is influenced by solutes, pressure, gravity, and the matric potential • Describe how water potential, evapotranspiration, and stomatal regulation influence how water is transported in plants • Explain how photosynthates are transported in plants The structure of plant roots, stems, and leaves facilitates the transport of water, nutrients, and photosynthates throughout the plant. The phloem and xylem are the main tissues responsible for this movement. Water potential, evapotranspiration, and stomatal regulation influence how water and nutrients are transported in plants. To understand how these processes work, we must first understand the energetics of water potential. Water Potential Plants are phenomenal hydraulic engineers. Using only the basic laws of physics and the simple manipulation of potential energy, plants can move water to the top of a 116-meter-tall tree (Figure 30.31a). Plants can also use hydraulics to generate enough force to split rocks and buckle sidewalks (Figure 30.31b). Plants achieve this because of water potential. 30.5 • Transport of Water and Solutes in Plants
> **[Figure 721, Page 907]** A wide diagram or timeline (1117×298px) related to: FIGURE, branches, mangrove, trees, develop, aerial. This image appears on page 907 of the textbook.
![Figure 721 - A wide diagram or timeline (1117×298px) related to: FIGURE, ](OpenStax_Biology_2e_images/img_0721_d53c6639.jpg)

FIGURE 30.32 In this example with a semipermeable membrane between two aqueous systems, water will move from a region of higher to lower water potential until equilibrium is reached. Solutes (Ψs), pressure (Ψp), and gravity (Ψg) influence total water potential for each side of the tube (Ψtotal right or left), and therefore, the difference between Ψtotal on each side (ΔΨ). (Ψm , the potential due to interaction of water with solid substrates, is ignored in this example because glass is not especially hydrophilic). Water moves in response to the difference in water potential between two systems (the left and right sides of the tube). Positive water potential is placed on the left side of the tube by increasing Ψp such that the water level rises on the right side. Could you equalize the water level on each side of the tube by adding solute, and if so, how? Pressure Potential Pressure potential (Ψp), also called turgor potential, may be positive or negative (Figure 30.32). Because pressure is an expression of energy, the higher the pressure, the more potential energy in a system, and vice versa. Therefore, a positive Ψp (compression) increases Ψtotal, and a negative Ψp (tension) decreases Ψtotal. Positive pressure inside cells is contained by the cell wall, producing turgor pressure. Pressure potentials are typically around 0.6–0.8 MPa, but can reach as high as 1.5 MPa in a well-watered plant. A Ψp of 1.5 MPa equates to 210 pounds per square inch (1.5 MPa x 140 lb/in-2 MPa-1 = 210 lb/in-2). As a comparison, most automobile tires are kept at a pressure of 30–34 psi. An example o

deep_worked_example.stem: 根据教材片段，设计一道关于「蒸腾作用」的现象解释或实验探究题。
deep_worked_example.solution_outline: 先描述现象或实验条件，再指出关键概念和证据，最后给出解释或结论。
deep_worked_example.evidence_excerpt: FIGURE 30.7 The central pith and outer cortex of the (a) flax stem are made up of parenchyma cells. Inside the cortex is a layer of sclerenchyma cells, which make up the fibers in flax rope and clothing. Humans have grown and harvested flax for thousands of ye

errors.description: 把「蒸腾作用」当作孤立事实记忆，不能说明结构、功能和生命过程之间的关系。
解释生命现象时缺少证据，容易把相关关系误认为因果关系。

exercises.stem: 用结构与功能或过程与结果的关系解释「蒸腾作用」。
exercises.answer: 应扣住课标要点：植物通过根吸收水分，通过导管运输水分，通过叶片散失水分，从而参与生物圈中的水循环。，说明生命现象、结构/过程和功能意义。

exercises.stem: 举一个生活、健康或生态案例，说明「蒸腾作用」的应用价值。
exercises.answer: 需写出案例、涉及的生物学原理以及对生活或环境的意义。

real_world: 观察人体健康、植物生长、生态环境或食品生产中与「蒸腾作用」相关的生命现象。
